items.q: Is profit shown on the balance sheet?
items.correct: Profit is calculated in the profit and loss account; the balance sheet shows assets, liabilities and equity at one date, with retained profit appearing within equity.
items.misconception: The balance sheet lists the year's revenues and costs.
items.random_wrong: The balance sheet records employee attendance.
items.why: Students mix up the two statements; knowing what each one shows is foundational.

items.q: Does positive cash flow prove a business is profitable?
items.correct: No — cash can rise from loans, share issues or selling assets while the firm trades at a loss; profitability and liquidity are different measures.
items.misconception: Money piling up in the bank means the business must be making profit.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow is simply profit after tax.
items.why: The cash-profit distinction works in both directions; students usually learn only one.

items.correct: No — depreciation spreads the cost of a fixed asset over its useful life in the accounts; no cash leaves the business when the charge is made.
items.misconception: Charging depreciation drains the bank account each year.
items.random_wrong: Depreciation is a fine paid for using old equipment.
items.why: Non-cash expenses confuse students when reconciling profit with cash flow.

items.q: Revenue $500k, cost of sales $200k. Gross profit is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: $300k. “$200k” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which statement is a snapshot at one date?
items.why: It's Balance sheet. “P&L” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Current assets $80k, current liabilities $30k. Working capital ($k) is:
items.why: The real figure is 50. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
